definitions.definition: to give all of your energy, time, etc.

definitions.definition: going or being a long way down from the top or surface, or being of a particular distance from the top to the bottom
examples: a deep well/mine
a deep river/sea
a deep cut
The hole is so deep you can't see the bottom.
The water's not deep here - look, I can touch the bottom.
Drill 20 holes, each 2 inches deep.
The water's only ankle/knee/waist-deep, so we'll be able to get across the river easily.
Take a few deep breaths (= breaths that fill the lungs with air) and calm down.

definitions.definition: very strongly felt or experienced and usually lasting a long time
examples: Their son has been a deep disappointment to them.
We're in deep trouble.
She fell into a deep sleep.

definitions.definition: (of a sound) low
examples: a wonderfully deep voice

definitions.definition: showing or needing serious thought, or not easy to understand
examples: His films are generally too deep for me.

definitions.definition: If something is deep, it has a large distance between its edges, especially between its front and back edges
examples: Is the alcove deep enough for bookshelves?
The wardrobe is 2 m high, 1 m wide and 60 cm deep.
By midnight, there were customers standing six deep (= in six rows) at the bar.

definitions.definition: near the middle of something, and a long distance from its edges
examples: Little Red Riding Hood's grandmother lived in a house deep in the forest.

definitions.definition: (of a colour) strong and dark
examples: The sky was deep blue.

definitions.definition: a long way from the top or surface
examples: The submarine sailed deep under the ice cap.
He thrust his hands deep in(to) his pockets.

definitions.definition: If a football player or team plays deep, they stay a long way inside their own half of the field
examples: Some midfielders play deep, having a more defensive role, while others go forward.

definitions.definition: the sea or the ocean
to make something deeper, or to become deeper
examples: One way of preventing further flooding would be to deepen the river bed.
The sea bed deepens here to 5,000 metres.

definitions.definition: to become more strongly felt or experienced, or to make something this way
examples: Over the years, her love for him deepened.
The economic crisis is deepening.
We must try not to deepen existing splits within the party.

definitions.definition: to make something increase or become more serious
examples: It certainly helped to deepen my understanding of the situation.

definitions.definition: to become lower
examples: Boys' voices deepen in their early to mid-teens.

definitions.definition: to become darker
examples: The shadows deepened as the sun dropped below the horizon.

definitions.definition: a failure to do something, such as pay a debt, that you legally have to do

examples: Napoleon was defeated by the Duke of Wellington at the battle of Waterloo.

definitions.definition: a reduction of the supply of money in an economy, and therefore a reduction of economic activity, that is often part of an intentional government plan to reduce prices
a reduction in value
examples: There has been a deflation in/of property values.

definitions.definition: connected with a reduction of the supply of money in an economy, and therefore a reduction of economic activity, that is often part of an intentional government plan to reduce prices
examples: a deflationary budget/policy
A stronger dollar will add to deflationary pressures in America.

definitions.definition: a reduction in prices in the economy as a whole, or for particular goods or services
examples: The grocery chain reported that it is feeling the effects of price deflation.
The clothing market has seen a price deflation of 2 to 3% a year.

definitions.definition: a figure that is used to change current prices or wages so that they can be compared to past prices or wages, by removing the effect of inflation
examples: The GDP deflator - the overall inflation measure covering all price changes in the economy and not just at the consumer level - rose 0.4 percent in the third quarter to 128.
The price deflator measures the magnitude of price increases and detects whether higher costs drive consumers away from a product.

definitions.definition: to change direction after hitting something, or to cause something to do this
examples: The ball deflected off my shoulder, straight into the goal.
The crowd cheered as the goalkeeper deflected the shot.
If a huge meteor was heading towards us, our best hope could be to deflect it away from the Earth using a nuclear explosion.

definitions.definition: to cause someone to change from or stop what they were intending to do
examples: His initial defeats did not deflect him from his ambitions.
She listens to him, but doesn't allow herself to be deflected from her purpose.
We will continue with our plans and not be deflected by the attacks.

definitions.definition: to avoid something such as criticism, blame, or a question being directed at you
examples: The president deflected criticism of his economic record by focusing on crime.
He successfully deflected blame for his mismanagement of the crisis.
I just tried to deflect questions the best way I could.

definitions.definition: to attack or blame another person rather than accepting criticism or blame for your own actions
examples: When someone deflects, they are trying to feel less guilty, avoid negative consequences, and put the blame on others.
The guilty person deflects their guilt onto the person who is accusing them or onto another person.

definitions.definition: past simple and past participle of deflect
to (cause to) change direction, or to prevent something from being directed at you
examples: The ball deflected off my shoulder, straight into the goal.
The crowd cheered as the goalkeeper deflected the shot.
He deflected the ball away from the goal.
The prime minister deflected mounting criticism today by announcing tax cuts.

definitions.definition: a change of direction after hitting something
examples: The second goal was from a deflection off the Liverpool defender.

definitions.definition: something you do or say in order to avoid something such as criticism, blame, or a question being directed at you
examples: Reporters were frustrated by her constant deflection of their questions.
I noticed his skilful deflection of blame onto other team members.
There are jokes and deflections but no answers.

definitions.definition: to refuse to obey a person, decision, law, situation, etc.
examples: It is rare to see children openly defying their teachers.
A few workers have defied the majority decision and gone into work despite the strike.

A forest fire raging in southern California is defying (= is not changed by) all attempts to control it.

definitions.definition: to be extreme or very strange and therefore impossible to believe, describe, or explain
examples: The chaos at the airport defies description.

I defy you to tell where I've painted over the scratch on my car.

definitions.definition: (an) amount or level of something
examples: This job demands a high degree of skill.
There isn't the slightest degree of doubt that he's innocent.
I have to warn you that there's a degree of (= some) danger involved in this.
The number of terrorist attacks has increased to a terrifying degree.
There was some degree of truth in what she said.
To what degree do you think we will be providing a better service?
"That's really bad." "Well, it's all a matter/question of degree (= there are other things better and other things worse)."

definitions.definition: any of various units of measurement, especially of temperature or angles, usually shown by the symbol ° written after a number
examples: a difference of three degrees
Water boils at 212° Fahrenheit and 100° Celsius/Centigrade.
A right angle is an angle of 90°.
New York is on a latitude of 41°N and a longitude of 74°W.

definitions.definition: a course of study at a college or university, or the qualification given to a student after he or she has completed his or her studies
examples: She has a physics degree/a degree in physics from Edinburgh.
She has a bachelor's/master's degree in history from Yale.
"What degree did you do at York?" "Geography."

definitions.definition: used with an ordinal number to show how serious something is
examples: She suffered first-/second-/third-degree (= least serious/serious/very serious) burns on her legs.
He's being charged with first-degree (= the most serious type of) murder.

definitions.definition: deleveraging

definitions.definition: to leave something somewhere

definitions.definition: to pay someone an amount of money when you make an agreement with that person to pay for or buy something, that either will be returned to you later, if the agreed arrangement is kept, or that forms part of the total payment
examples: When we moved in, we had to deposit $1,000 with the landlord in case we broke any of his things.
You deposit 20 percent now and pay the rest when the car is delivered.

definitions.definition: a substance or layer that is left, usually after a liquid is removed
examples: Decant the wine carefully, so that you leave the deposit in the bottom of the bottle.

examples: mineral/oil/coal deposits

definitions.definition: a payment, especially into a bank account
examples: To open an account, you need to make a minimum deposit of $500.

definitions.definition: an amount of money that you pay as the first part of the total payment for something
examples: The shop assistant says if I leave £10 as a deposit, they'll keep the dress for me.

They asked us to put down a deposit.

definitions.definition: an amount of money that you pay when you rent something, and is returned to you when you return the thing you have rented
examples: It costs $2,000 a week to rent the yacht, with a $200 refundable/returnable deposit.

Jane descended the stairs.

definitions.definition: If darkness or night descends, it becomes dark and day changes to night.
If a negative or bad feeling descends, it is felt everywhere in a place or by everyone at the same time
examples: A feeling of despair descended (on us) as we realized that we were lost.

definitions.definition: If a condition, usually a negative condition, descends, it quickly develops in every part of a place
examples: Silence descended on the room/over the countryside.

definitions.definition: the state or fact of being related to a particular person or group of people who lived in the past
examples: She's a woman of mixed/French descent.
They trace their line of descent back to a French duke.
He claims direct descent from Mohammed.

definitions.definition: an occasion when a group of people arrive somewhere, usually suddenly or unexpectedly
examples: We weren't prepared for the descent of thousands of journalists on the town.

definitions.definition: a movement down
examples: The plane began (to make) its final descent into the airport.
There is a steep descent (= way down, such as a path) to the village below.

definitions.definition: a change in someone's behaviour, or in a situation, from good to bad
examples: His descent into crime was rapid.

definitions.definition: to say or write what someone or something is like
examples: Could you describe your attacker?
He described the painting in detail.
Let me describe (to you) how it happened.
She described Gary as shy.

definitions.definition: If you describe a shape, you draw it or move in a direction that follows the line of it
examples: He used compasses to describe a circle.

definitions.definition: to recognize a plant or animal as belonging to a particular type, and give it a scientific name
examples: So far, scientists have described 15,482 marine fish species, up from 15,304 a year ago.
The scientific name for mountain laurel is Kalmia latifolia, from Peter Kalm, an 18th-century botanist who first described the species.

definitions.definition: to see or notice something or someone
examples: Miss Bates, passing near the window, descried Mr Knightley on horseback not far off.

examples: Miss Bates, passing near the window, descried Mr Knightley on horseback not far off.

definitions.definition: something that tells you what something or someone is like
examples: Write a description of your favourite beach.
She has given the police a very detailed/full description of the robber.
A girl answering (= matching) the description of the missing teenager was spotted in Glasgow.
Your paper contains too much description, and not enough discussion of the issues.

definitions.definition: of all types
examples: Boats of every description were entering the harbour.

definitions.definition: to be something that you cannot describe accurately because of its great size, quality, or level
examples: Her beauty is beyond description.
The mess in Bart's room defies description (= is very bad)!

examples: a descriptive essay/passage

definitions.definition: A descriptive area of study is one that is based on saying what its subject is really like, rather than on developing theories about it
examples: descriptive linguistics/sociology/statistics

definitions.definition: the belief that books about language should describe how language is really used, rather than giving rules to follow saying what is correct and not correct
examples: There has been a blurring of the sharp distinction between descriptivism and prescriptivism in the writings of grammarians and lexicographers.

definitions.definition: believing that books about language should describe how language is really used, rather than giving rules to follow saying what is correct and not correct
examples: He condemned the descriptivist view of language as an "Anything Goes" school.
We linguists tend to be descriptivist.

definitions.definition: someone who believes that books about language should describe how language is really used, rather than giving rules to follow saying what is correct and not correct
examples: He was a descriptivist and believed that a dictionary should reflect the actual contemporary state of the language.

definitions.definition: to damage or show no respect towards something holy or very much respected
examples: The mosque/shrine was desecrated by vandals.
It's a crime to desecrate the country's flag.

definitions.definition: a small, round or oval object produced by a plant and from which, when it is planted, a new plant can grow
examples: Sow the seeds (= put them in the ground) about three centimetres deep.
The chemical will stop all seeds from sprouting (= starting to grow).
The farmers grow these crops for seed (= for planting to grow more crops, rather than for eating).

definitions.definition: → semen
If a food plant goes or runs to seed, it produces flowers and seeds because it has not been picked early enough
examples: In hot weather lettuces can suddenly run to seed.

definitions.definition: If people or places go or run to seed, their physical appearance becomes worse because no one is taking care of them
examples: After he retired, he really went to seed.

definitions.definition: the cause of a feeling or situation, or the early stages of it
examples: From these early seeds of their friendship, they grew into lifelong companions.
He may be sowing the seeds of his own destruction in the long term by using violence against his own people.

definitions.definition: especially in tennis, a good player who is given a place on the list of those expected to win games in a particular competition because of the way they have played in the past
examples: Turner's opponent in the quarter-finals is the number one seed.

definitions.definition: to produce seeds
examples: The plants have seeded themselves (= their seeds have fallen) into the cracks between the paving stones.

definitions.definition: to remove the seeds from a fruit or vegetable
examples: Wash, seed, and cut the pepper into small pieces.

definitions.definition: to sow (= plant) seeds somewhere
examples: Winter wheat can be seeded into open plots.
Spring is a great time to seed the bare patches in your lawn.

definitions.definition: to cause something to exist and develop
examples: This was the article that seeded his book.
The main strategy was getting others to buy into a vision, seeding ideas at different levels in the organization.

definitions.definition: to make a player a seed
examples: Jones, seeded second, has won her last ten matches.

definitions.definition: to spread substances such as certain chemicals in clouds, in order to try to make them produce rain, or more rain
examples: Scientists have experimented with cloud control, including seeding clouds to promote the shedding of rain.
He seeds clouds in west Texas.

examples: They were lost in the desert for nine days.
We had to cross a large area of arid, featureless desert.
the desert sun

definitions.definition: a place that is considered to have no cultural, intellectual, etc. quality or interest
examples: This town is a cultural desert.

definitions.definition: to leave the armed forces without permission and with no intention of returning
examples: Soldiers who deserted and were caught were shot.
How many people desert from the army each year?

definitions.definition: to leave someone without help or in a difficult situation and not come back
examples: He deserted his wife and family for another woman.

definitions.definition: If a quality deserts you, you suddenly and temporarily lose it
examples: All my confidence/courage deserted me when I walked into the exam.

definitions.definition: If a place is deserted, there are no people in it
examples: a deserted building/street
The coastal resorts are deserted in winter.

definitions.definition: left alone in a difficult situation

definitions.definition: to get what you deserve
examples: I'd say he got his just deserts for not sticking around to help with the kids.

definitions.definition: to have earned or to be given something because of the way you have behaved or the qualities you have
examples: After all that hard work, you deserve a holiday.
Chris deserves our special thanks for all his efforts.
I hope they get the punishment they deserve.
They certainly deserved to win that game.

definitions.definition: used to refer to something that you earn or are given because of your behaviour or qualities

definitions.definition: to deserve to get something

definitions.definition: a person who comes from or whose family comes from India, Pakistan, or Bangladesh but who lives in another country
examples: Urban desis are mixing Hindi and hip-hop.
There were only a handful of other desis in the area when he was in elementary school.

definitions.definition: in a traditional or home-made Indian style
examples: All Pilau dishes are served with fresh salad and special desi chutney.
Shaami kebab is our desi version of a burger.

definitions.definition: a substance used to remove moisture (= liquid in the form of small drops) from something

definitions.definition: to remove the moisture from something so it becomes completely dry; to lose all moisture and become completely dry
examples: Intensive farming has desiccated the land.

definitions.definition: to become damaged or destroyed by losing an important quality; to damage or destroy something in this way

definitions.definition: dried, with the moisture removed

definitions.definition: an activity you do for entertainment

definitions.definition: a system that allows someone to avoid having a permanent criminal record, or to avoid punishment as a criminal, if they complete a course of treatment or study
examples: Pretrial diversion is a program for certain first-time offenders to give them a chance to avoid being marked by a felony conviction for the rest of their lives.
The diversion program will remove a shoplifting incident from her daughter's record.

definitions.definition: taking your attention away from something else

I'm always dropping things.
Amanda dropped her sunglasses in/into the fountain.
The book dropped from/off the shelf.
Don't drop it!/Don't let it drop!

definitions.definition: to die suddenly and unexpectedly
examples: He dropped dead on the squash court at the age of 43.

definitions.definition: to write someone a letter, especially a short informal one
examples: Just drop me a line when you decide on a date.

definitions.definition: to tell someone something in a way that is not direct
examples: She dropped a hint that she'd like to come to the party.

definitions.definition: to not pronounce the letter h at the beginning of words in which it should be pronounced
to move to a lower level, or cause something to move to a lower level
examples: The water level in the flooded region has finally begun to drop.
The land drops (away) (= slopes down) sharply behind the barrier.
We had to drop our prices because of the recession.

definitions.definition: to stop doing or planning something, especially an activity
examples: I'm going to drop yoga and do aerobics instead.
Can you drop what you're doing and help me with this report?

definitions.definition: to stop including someone in a group or team
examples: He's been dropped from the team because of injury.

definitions.definition: to take someone to a particular place, usually in a car, and leave them there
examples: They dropped me off at the main entrance.
I dropped him at the library and went shopping.

definitions.definition: to become available for people to buy, listen to, or watch, especially using the internet; to make something available like this
examples: It's an exciting time for her, with the tour starting and the record about to drop.
We heard that rapper Dave was about to drop a new album.

definitions.definition: a small round-shaped amount of liquid
examples: I thought I felt a drop of rain.
There were little drops of paint on the kitchen floor.

definitions.definition: a small amount of liquid you can drink
examples: I'll have a drop more juice, please.
"Would you like some milk?" "Just a drop, please."

definitions.definition: liquid medicine given in very small amounts
examples: eye/nose/ear drops

definitions.definition: a small piece of sweet food made of sugar
examples: fruit/pear drops
chocolate drops

definitions.definition: the distance from one thing to something lower
examples: There's a drop of two metres from the window to the ground.

definitions.definition: a reduction in the amount or level of something
examples: The recent drop in magazine subscriptions is causing some concern.

definitions.definition: the act of delivering things such as supplies, medicine, etc., often by dropping them from an aircraft
examples: a food drop
The helicopter made a drop of much-needed supplies to the stranded hikers.

definitions.definition: the situation in which something is left in a place and later collected by someone else, usually a spy
a small drop of liquid
a small drop of liquid
a person who leaves school, college, or university before completing a qualification, or a person who lives in an unusual way
examples: a high school/college dropout

examples: I hung his wet trousers on the radiator, but they're not dry yet.

This cake's a bit dry - I think I left it in the oven for too long.

definitions.definition: used to describe weather or periods of time when no rain falls
examples: If it's dry tomorrow I'll cut the grass.
It should be a nice, sunny, dry day.

definitions.definition: If a river or other area of water runs dry, the water gradually disappears from it
examples: By this time all the wells had run dry.

definitions.definition: Dry hair or skin does not have enough of the natural oils that make it soft and smooth

definitions.definition: Dry bread is plain, without butter, jam, etc.
examples: All I was offered was a piece of dry bread and an apple!

definitions.definition: If a book, talk, subject, etc. is dry, it is not interesting.

definitions.definition: Dry humour is very funny in a way that is clever and not obvious

definitions.definition: to give something or someone a particular name, especially describing what you think of it, him, or her

definitions.definition: to change the sounds and speech on a film or television programme, especially to a different language
